title: 0.68 not compatible with podman 5.8.2, but required for buildah 1.44.0?
body:
Hi! 👋 

I package this project for Arch Linux.

To prevent breakage, we check against versions of podman when building the package, as the provided configuration files may break some of its consumers.
As per your past recommendation, we are (still) checking against the minor version of this project used in the podman project to prevent breakage:
https://gitlab.archlinux.org/archlinux/packaging/packages/containers-common/-/blob/61043aecefa98cd2f1bc7a2bf9ed759fe31fe912/PKGBUILD#L94-106 

However, we have now arrived at a weird spot. It appears buildah requires 0.68, while current podman (5.8.2) still requires 0.67:
https://github.com/podman-container-tools/buildah/issues/6880

It would be great if these version requirements could be kept in sync, as it is already quite challenging to keep this in sync (manually).
If the requirements have changed, please let me know!

comments.body:
Thanks for your report.

We are in the process of releasing Podman 6.0; due to various infrastructure changes (notably Cirrus CI going away) that has taken longer than anticipated, and we are in the middle of that. Buildah 1.44 and Podman 6.0 are both targeting the updated configuration semantics. Podman 5.8.x should, IIRC, pair with Buildah 1.43.x.

Podman 6.0 rc1 should arrive within a few days.
Thanks for the rough timeline! I think we will downgrade buildah for the time being to wait for podman to be released and try to improve our safeguards.

FWIW all of buildah 1.44, podman 6.0, skopeo 1.23 will use the new config file logic. So they all must be updated together with the the config file changes.

Example rpm changes for the config files: https://github.com/podman-container-tools/container-libs/pull/795

In addition buildah 1.44 and podman 6.0 require netavark 2.0 (not yet released, should happen very soon) so this must be updated together with the rest as well.

I don't know how it works in the Arch world but I would expect all these packages to be updated as part of one transaction so users do not end up in state where one needs the old config files while another tools needs the new config files.

> so this must be updated together with the rest as well.

Thanks for the heads up!

> I don't know how it works in the Arch world but I would expect all these packages to be updated as part of one transaction so users do not end up in state where one needs the old config files while another tools needs the new config files.

That's usually what we do.
However, the package sources for containers-common, podman, buildah, netavark, etc. are all distinct and testing that they work with each other tends to be either manual or not easily possible.
We currently only test compatibility of containers-common with podman, but I guess we'll extend it to buildah and also try to test the reverse in some way.

> FWIW all of buildah 1.44, podman 6.0, skopeo 1.23 will use the new config file logic. So they all must be updated together with the the config file changes.

Where was this communicated? I don't see any changelogs or breaking change announcements in the respective upstreams?

At least https://github.com/podman-container-tools/skopeo/releases/tag/v1.23.0 and https://github.com/podman-container-tools/buildah/releases/tag/v1.44.0 contain a short note; I think a longer announcement is planned.

I’m afraid this release cycle is messier than usual.

Somewhat related: cri-o also depends on common, but I'm not sure if it would be affected by changes to e.g. configuration files as much.
Either way: The version they track is much older 🫠 
https://github.com/cri-o/cri-o/blob/75a79851f912e67647903fddddcb65ae1bfdabeb/go.mod#L69

> Somewhat related: cri-o also depends on common, but I'm not sure if it would be affected by changes to e.g. configuration files as much.

They don't depend on containers.conf but cri-o does use storage.conf, and image config files, registries.conf, policy.json,etc...
I did test the storage.conf changes with crio and it worked fine.

From my local dev viewpoint I think the only major breaking change was that graphroot/runroot should be commented out and the build in code should pick the right defaults. Otherwise the new code should in theory still work with the old files.
And if you comment out graphroot/runroot from storage.conf it should still work fine with the older code.

The main change that will not work will the older code is moving the files to /usr/share/containers, but because we still check /etc/containers as well there is no hard requirement to move them right now, though I would say it would be nice if all the packaged files are under /usr and leave /etc for admin configs but if you need to wait for all the consumers to get updated to the new code that is understandable.

Oh and just in case it was missed, I released the required netavark and aardvark-dns this week.
https://github.com/containers/netavark/releases/tag/v2.0.0
https://github.com/containers/aardvark-dns/releases/tag/v2.0.0

> Oh and just in case it was missed, I released the required netavark and aardvark-dns this week.

Thanks, I saw those already (waiting for podman 6 to be released to upgrade those two).

We released https://github.com/podman-container-tools/podman/releases/tag/v6.0.0-rc1

I know you likely do not want/should ship an rc1 in the main arch repos but I guess it could be nice if you could try to build it and see if the latest podman/buildah/skopeo/netavark/aardvark-dns/containers-common combination works as expected now.

I've built the entire stack and published a test repository on pkgbuild.com (our file host). All signed with my packager key.

https://pkgbuild.com/~foxboron/repos/podman/

A simple test with `rc1` seems to work fine!

FYI, per our current planning unless critical problems come up with rc1 we plan for 6.0.0 final on June 24th.
@Luap99 Is there anything we should help test with the current package set? If you give a couple of pointers I don't mind writing an email to our development list to get some more input.

I mean the obvious thing is run your normal podman workflows and make sure you do not have any regressions that are not documented on the release notes.

Otherwise for the config file stuff well out the remaining files from /etc to /usr and ensure they get picked up. i.e. your pull output shows
> Resolved "archlinux" as an alias (/etc/containers/registries.conf.d/00-shortnames.conf)

if you move that to /usr/share/containers/registries.conf.d/00-shortnames.conf does that get picked up as it should, etc...
I am working a blog post with more details about the config changes.
Ok! I'll reorganize the `containers-common` package and move the files from `/etc` into `/usr/share`. I'll also write an email and encourage more users.

yes but note https://github.com/podman-container-tools/container-libs/issues/902#issuecomment-4698632170

If you package other applications that still use the older container-libs releases you will most likely break them, at least the policy.json file must be present for pulls to work.
I'll add cri-o and do a round of testing. I assume this is going to be practical knowledge for other downstreams as well.
blog about the config changes: https://blog.podman.io/2026/06/podman-6-configuration-file-changes/

Podman 6 is released https://github.com/podman-container-tools/podman/releases/tag/v6.0.0

I am going to close this, I hope the dependency situation is clear now, if you find bugs you know where to file new issues.
